(né **Jeffrey**; 1 March 1947 – 13 December 2016) was a Canadian-American actor, songwriter, and game/talk show host. He was the father of singer [Robin Thicke](/wiki/Robin_Thicke "Robin Thicke"). Thicke was best known for playing Dr. Jason Seaver on the 1980s sitcom *[Growing Pains](/wiki/Growing_Pains "Growing Pains")* on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"). In 2013, he was inducted into [Canada's Walk of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame "Canada's Walk of Fame").

**Alan Willis Thicke** (né **Jeffrey**; 1 March 1947 – 13 December 2016) was a Canadian-American actor, songwriter, and game/talk show host. He was the father of singer [Robin Thicke](/wiki/Robin_Thicke "Robin Thicke"). Thicke was best known for playing Dr. Jason Seaver on the 1980s sitcom *[Growing Pains](/wiki/Growing_Pains "Growing Pains")* on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"). In 2013, he was inducted into [Canada's Walk of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame "Canada's Walk of Fame").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Thicke was born on 1 March 1947 in [Kirkland Lake](/wiki/Kirkland_Lake "Kirkland Lake"), [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), the son of Shirley "Joan" Isobel Marie ([née](/wiki/Birth_name#Maiden_and_married_names "Birth name") Greer), a nurse, and William Jeffrey, a stockbroker.[[2]](#cite_note-filmref-2) They divorced in 1953. His mother remarried Brian Thicke, a physician, and they moved to [Elliot Lake](/wiki/Elliot_Lake "Elliot Lake").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Taking his stepfather's name, Alan Thicke graduated from [Elliot Lake Secondary School](/wiki/Elliot_Lake_Secondary_School "Elliot Lake Secondary School") in 1965.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He went on to attend the [University of Western Ontario](/wiki/University_of_Western_Ontario "University of Western Ontario"), joining the [Delta Upsilon](/wiki/Delta_Upsilon "Delta Upsilon") fraternity.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Hosting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Hosting")]

#### Game shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Game shows")]

Thicke hosted the Canadian game show *Face the Music* for [CHCH-TV](/wiki/CHCH-TV "CHCH-TV") by Niagara Television in 1975 (not affiliated with Sandy Frank Productions' [1980-81 version](/wiki/Face_the_Music_(American_game_show) "Face the Music (American game show)")). He hosted the Canadian game show *First Impressions* on the CTV network during the 1976–77 season, taped at [CFCF-TV](/wiki/CFCF-TV "CFCF-TV") in Montreal,[[6]](#cite_note-6) the Saturday morning celebrity game show *[Animal Crack-Ups](/wiki/Animal_Crack-Ups "Animal Crack-Ups")* in the late 1980s,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and, in 1997, a television version of the board game *[Pictionary](/wiki/Pictionary_(1997_game_show) "Pictionary (1997 game show)")*.[[2]](#cite_note-filmref-2) In the early 2000s, he hosted the *[All New 3's a Crowd](/wiki/Three%27s_a_Crowd_(game_show) "Three's a Crowd (game show)")* on the [Game Show Network](/wiki/Game_Show_Network "Game Show Network").[[2]](#cite_note-filmref-2)

#### Talk shows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Talk shows")]

[Norman Lear](/wiki/Norman_Lear "Norman Lear") hired Thicke to produce and head the writing staff of *[Fernwood 2 Night](/wiki/Fernwood_2_Night "Fernwood 2 Night")*, a tongue-in-cheek talk show based on characters from Lear's earlier show, *[Mary Hartman, Mary Hartman](/wiki/Mary_Hartman,_Mary_Hartman "Mary Hartman, Mary Hartman")*.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) In the late 1970s, he was a frequent guest host of *The Alan Hamel Show*, a popular daytime talk show on Canadian TV, usually hosted by [Alan Hamel](/wiki/Alan_Hamel "Alan Hamel").[[9]](#cite_note-:1-9) When the Hamel series ended in the early 1980s, it was replaced by *[The Alan Thicke Show](/wiki/The_Alan_Thicke_Show "The Alan Thicke Show")*. The show at one point spawned a prime-time spinoff, *Prime Cuts*, which consisted of edited highlights from the talk show.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Thicke was later signed to do an American syndicated late-night talk show, *[Thicke of the Night](/wiki/Thicke_of_the_Night "Thicke of the Night")*, for the 1983–1984 TV season. Heavily promoted prior to broadcast as a competitor to NBC's *[The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson](/wiki/The_Tonight_Show_Starring_Johnny_Carson "The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson")*, *Thicke of the Night* was short-lived.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

### Producing and composing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Producing and composing")]

Thicke had a successful career as a TV theme song composer, often collaborating with his then-wife Gloria Loring on these projects, which included the themes to the popular sitcoms *[Diff'rent Strokes](/wiki/Diff%27rent_Strokes "Diff'rent Strokes")* and *[The Facts of Life](/wiki/The_Facts_of_Life_(TV_series) "The Facts of Life (TV series)")*.[[9]](#cite_note-:1-9) He also wrote a number of TV game show themes, including *[The Wizard of Odds](/wiki/The_Wizard_of_Odds "The Wizard of Odds")* (for which he also sang the vocal introduction),[[10]](#cite_note-10) *[The Joker's Wild](/wiki/The_Joker%27s_Wild "The Joker's Wild")*, *[Celebrity Sweepstakes](/wiki/Celebrity_Sweepstakes "Celebrity Sweepstakes")*, *[The Diamond Head Game](/wiki/The_Diamond_Head_Game "The Diamond Head Game")*, *Animal Crack-Ups* (which he co-wrote with his brother [Todd Thicke](/wiki/Todd_Thicke "Todd Thicke") and Gary Pickus), *[Blank Check](/wiki/Blank_Check_(game_show) "Blank Check (game show)")*, *[Stumpers!](/wiki/Stumpers! "Stumpers!")*, *[Whew!](/wiki/Whew! "Whew!")*,[[11]](#cite_note-tvpmm-whew-credits-11) and the original theme to *[Wheel of Fortune](/wiki/Wheel_of_Fortune_(American_game_show) "Wheel of Fortune (American game show)")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Thicke was a popular songwriter. He co-wrote "Sara", a solo hit for [Bill Champlin](/wiki/Bill_Champlin "Bill Champlin") and included on the latter's *[Runaway](/wiki/Runaway_(Bill_Champlin_album) "Runaway (Bill Champlin album)")* album (1981).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Thicke produced a variety of television shows, including [Anne Murray](/wiki/Anne_Murray "Anne Murray") Christmas specials for the [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation"), beginning in the late 1970s.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### *Growing Pains*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Growing Pains")]

Thicke became well known when he played Jason Seaver, a psychiatrist and father, on the family sitcom *[Growing Pains](/wiki/Growing_Pains_(TV_series) "Growing Pains (TV series)")*.[[15]](#cite_note-NYtimes_bio-15) When the show began, Jason was moving his psychiatry practice into the home to be closer to the family's children while the family matriarch Maggie, played by [Joanna Kerns](/wiki/Joanna_Kerns "Joanna Kerns"), resumed her career as a reporter.[[16]](#cite_note-Margulies,_Never_too_Thicke-16) *Growing Pains* debuted on ABC in 1985 and ran until 1992.[[15]](#cite_note-NYtimes_bio-15)[[17]](#cite_note-Slate,_A_Night_of_Long-17) For his role, Thicke was nominated for a [Golden Globe](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award "Golden Globe Award") award in 1988.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Thicke reprised his role in two reunion TV movies, *[The Growing Pains Movie](/wiki/The_Growing_Pains_Movie "The Growing Pains Movie")* (2000)[[18]](#cite_note-18) and *[Growing Pains: Return of the Seavers](/wiki/Growing_Pains:_Return_of_the_Seavers "Growing Pains: Return of the Seavers")* (2004).[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Television and film appearances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Television and film appearances")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/CFC_in_LA_%28Alan_Thicke%29.jpg/250px-CFC_in_LA_%28Alan_Thicke%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:CFC_in_LA_(Alan_Thicke).jpg)

Thicke at a [Canadian Film Centre](/wiki/Canadian_Film_Centre "Canadian Film Centre") and Variety-hosted reception for the [Telefilm Canada](/wiki/Telefilm_Canada "Telefilm Canada") Features Comedy Lab, March 2012

Thicke co-hosted the [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World "Walt Disney World") Very Merry Christmas Parade (now the [Disney Parks Christmas Day Parade](/wiki/Disney_Parks_Christmas_Day_Parade "Disney Parks Christmas Day Parade")) with [Joan Lunden](/wiki/Joan_Lunden "Joan Lunden") from 1983 to 1990, when he was succeeded by [Regis Philbin](/wiki/Regis_Philbin "Regis Philbin").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Thicke also hosted the 1987 and 1988 Crystal Light National Aerobic Championships.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In 1987, Thicke appeared as Dr. [Jonas Carson](/wiki/Jonas_Carson "Jonas Carson"), who creates an [android](/wiki/Android_(robot) "Android (robot)") that looks just like a human teenage boy (played by [Jay Underwood](/wiki/Jay_Underwood "Jay Underwood")), and he "adopts" him as his son in the [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel") film *[Not Quite Human](/wiki/Not_Quite_Human_(film) "Not Quite Human (film)")*. Thicke reprised his role as Jonas Carson in two sequels, 1989's *[Not Quite Human II](/wiki/Not_Quite_Human_II "Not Quite Human II")* and 1992's *[Still Not Quite Human](/wiki/Still_Not_Quite_Human "Still Not Quite Human")*.

In 1988, he hosted the [Miss USA](/wiki/Miss_USA "Miss USA") Pageant in [El Paso, Texas](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"), replacing [Bob Barker](/wiki/Bob_Barker "Bob Barker") (who quit over fur being involved in the pageants).[[23]](#cite_note-23) He replaced Barker again as host of the 1988 [Miss Universe](/wiki/Miss_Universe "Miss Universe") Pageant (along with [Tracy Scoggins](/wiki/Tracy_Scoggins "Tracy Scoggins")) in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), Taiwan.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Thicke was replaced by [Dick Clark](/wiki/Dick_Clark "Dick Clark") as host of the 1989 Miss USA Pageant in [Mobile](/wiki/Mobile,_Alabama "Mobile, Alabama"), Alabama[[25]](#cite_note-25) and by [John Forsythe](/wiki/John_Forsythe "John Forsythe") as host of the 1989 Miss Universe Pageant in [Cancún](/wiki/Canc%C3%BAn "Cancún"), Mexico.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Thicke continued to host a wide range of variety TV events. In 1989, he co-hosted with [SCTV](/wiki/Second_City_Television "Second City Television") alumna [Andrea Martin](/wiki/Andrea_Martin "Andrea Martin") the TV special *Opening of [SkyDome](/wiki/SkyDome "SkyDome")* in Toronto, which aired across Canada on the [CBC](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) In 2004, he hosted the [Miss Universe Canada](/wiki/Miss_Universe_Canada "Miss Universe Canada") Pageant in Ontario.

In 1992, Thicke appeared as himself in the pilot episode of the sitcom *[Hangin' with Mr. Cooper](/wiki/Hangin%27_with_Mr._Cooper "Hangin' with Mr. Cooper")*. He appeared in the end-credits scene, alongside series star [Mark Curry](/wiki/Mark_Curry_(actor) "Mark Curry (actor)"), humorously referencing the pilot episode being filmed on the same set used as the Seavers' home on *Growing Pains*. He also made guest appearances on shows such as *[Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote")*; *[Married... with Children](/wiki/Married..._with_Children "Married... with Children")*; *[Son of the Beach](/wiki/Son_of_the_Beach "Son of the Beach")* and *[7th Heaven](/wiki/7th_Heaven_(TV_series) "7th Heaven (TV series)")*.

From 1995 to 1996, Thicke appeared on the American television series *[Hope & Gloria](/wiki/Hope_%26_Gloria "Hope & Gloria")*, which ran for 35 episodes.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In March 2002, Thicke participated in a celebrity version of *[Fear Factor](/wiki/Fear_Factor "Fear Factor")*. In May 2002, he appeared in the season six finale of *[Just Shoot Me](/wiki/Just_Shoot_Me "Just Shoot Me")*, "The Boys in the Band."[[30]](#cite_note-30) From 2006 to 2009, Thicke was talk show host Rich Ginger on *[The Bold and the Beautiful](/wiki/The_Bold_and_the_Beautiful "The Bold and the Beautiful").*[[31]](#cite_note-31)

From 2001 to 2003, Thicke hosted *[Animal Miracles](/wiki/Animal_Miracles "Animal Miracles")* on the [Pax TV](/wiki/ION_Television "ION Television") network. In April 2006, he hosted *[Celebrity Cooking Showdown](/wiki/Celebrity_Cooking_Showdown "Celebrity Cooking Showdown")* on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), in which celebrities were teamed with famous chefs in a cooking competition.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

#### Commercials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Commercials")]

In the 1990s, Thicke was the spokesman for the Canadian division of [Woolco](/wiki/Woolco "Woolco") department stores until its demise in 1994.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In 2007, Thicke appeared in a television ad for [Tahiti Village](/wiki/Tahiti_Village "Tahiti Village"), a Las Vegas [time-share](/wiki/Time-share "Time-share") resort.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 2009, Thicke began appearing in TV ads endorsing CCS Medical, a distributor of home-delivered diabetes supplies.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] In 2014, he began representing Optima Tax Relief.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

From 2011, Thicke was the spokesperson for Cambridge Life Solutions, a Canadian company that promised to reduce [unsecured consumer debt](/wiki/Unsecured_debt "Unsecured debt") through a method known as [debt settlement](/wiki/Debt_settlement "Debt settlement"), which had been outlawed in the United States by the [Federal Trade Commission](/wiki/Federal_Trade_Commission "Federal Trade Commission") as a predatory practice in 2010 and was subsequently banned in Ontario in 2015. According to Scott Hannah, the president and CEO of the Credit Counseling Society of Canada, the company, which was accused of "bilking thousands of vulnerable Canadians" eventually dominated half of the Canadian market due to Thicke's hiring "as a spokesman who was very credible to Canadians."[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Thicke was married three times: His first marriage, to *[Days of Our Lives](/wiki/Days_of_Our_Lives "Days of Our Lives")* actress [Gloria Loring](/wiki/Gloria_Loring "Gloria Loring"), lasted from 1970 until 1984; they had two sons, Brennan and [Robin](/wiki/Robin_Thicke "Robin Thicke").[[2]](#cite_note-filmref-2) In 1987 at the age of 40, Thicke began dating 17-year old [Kristy Swanson](/wiki/Kristy_Swanson "Kristy Swanson"). Two years later, they were engaged but never married. He married his second wife, [Miss World 1990](/wiki/Miss_World_1990 "Miss World 1990") [Gina Tolleson](/wiki/Gina_Tolleson "Gina Tolleson"), on 13 August 1994, and had a son, Carter William Thicke, before their divorce was finalized on 29 September 1999.[[2]](#cite_note-filmref-2) In 1999, he met Tanya Callau in Miami, where he was a celebrity host and she was a model.[[51]](#cite_note-51) They were married from 2005 until his death.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Thicke lent his name and star power to supporting the Alan Thicke Center for diabetes research.[[53]](#cite_note-53) Also, for several years in the mid-1980s, Thicke and Gloria Loring were co-hosts of [Telemiracle](/wiki/Telemiracle "Telemiracle"), an annual 20-hour [telethon](/wiki/Telethon "Telethon") that alternated between [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon") and [Regina, Saskatchewan](/wiki/Regina,_Saskatchewan "Regina, Saskatchewan"), to support programs run by the [Kinsmen Club](/wiki/Kinsmen_Club "Kinsmen Club").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alan_Thicke&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Death")]

On 13 December 2016, Thicke collapsed while playing ice hockey with his son Carter at Pickwick Gardens in [Burbank, California](/wiki/Burbank,_California "Burbank, California"). The manager of the rink said he was talking and even joked to his son to take a photo as he was being wheeled out on a stretcher.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Thicke died later that day of type-A [aortic dissection](/wiki/Aortic_dissection "Aortic dissection") at the [Providence Saint Joseph Medical Center](/wiki/Providence_Saint_Joseph_Medical_Center "Providence Saint Joseph Medical Center") in Burbank, at age 69.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) On 19 December 2016, the cast of *Growing Pains*, including [Leonardo DiCaprio](/wiki/Leonardo_DiCaprio "Leonardo DiCaprio"), reunited at Thicke's funeral; a eulogy was given by his friend [Bob Saget](/wiki/Bob_Saget "Bob Saget"), and his son, [Robin](/wiki/Robin_Thicke "Robin Thicke"), offered a humorous remembrance. He was buried at Santa Barbara Cemetery in [Santa Barbara, California](/wiki/Santa_Barbara,_California "Santa Barbara, California").[[57]](#cite_note-57)
